jokes.text: I got a cactus, for God's sakes. That's a universal sign for 'things are over.'
jokes.setup: Michael trying to rationalize why he thinks Rebel knows they broke up
jokes.punchline: The absurd logic that a cactus is a universal breakup signal
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael Bluth overthinking with pseudo-logical reasoning. The specificity of 'for God's sakes' and 'universal sign' elevates it above basic absurdity.

jokes.text: You take my bag of lacy ticklers, hoo-ha bras, dog toys, and sex knobs
jokes.setup: Someone giving advice about appearing sexually confident
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous euphemisms for sex toys, especially 'hoo-ha bras' and mixing in 'dog toys'
jokes.explanation: The creative euphemisms and the casual inclusion of 'dog toys' in a list of sex items creates absurd humor typical of AD.

jokes.text: he decided to be the adult in the relationship and avoid conflict by squatting in a bush
jokes.setup: Narrator explaining Michael's mature decision-making
jokes.punchline: The contradiction between 'being the adult' and literally hiding in bushes
jokes.explanation: Perfect narrator irony - the formal tone describing childish behavior. Classic AD juxtaposition of sophisticated language with ridiculous actions.

jokes.text: Yeah, standing out here with a bag of sex toys! Let's go! Not getting any less horny out here.
jokes.setup: George Michael trying to act confident and sexual
jokes.punchline: The awkward oversharing and crude directness from normally innocent George Michael
jokes.explanation: The humor comes from George Michael being wildly out of character - his innocent nature makes the crude talk particularly cringe-funny.

jokes.text: You know, there's a woman in there who's obsessed with me... Oh, this one, too?
jokes.setup: Michael trying to explain why he's hiding in bushes
jokes.punchline: The cop's weary recognition that this happens to Michael frequently
jokes.explanation: The cop's casual 'this one, too?' suggests this is a recurring pattern for Michael, perfect character consistency callback.

jokes.text: That is one dirty cop. Dad.
jokes.setup: George Michael seeing his father emerge from bushes with a police officer
jokes.punchline: The double meaning of 'dirty cop' - corrupt vs. literally covered in dirt
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay. The economy is excellent - just four words that work on multiple levels.

jokes.text: I could run that up for you. Save you a trip. Make sure she doesn't think you're trying to break up.
jokes.setup: George Michael offering to deliver his father's breakup message
jokes.punchline: The reverse psychology attempt while actively trying to sabotage his father
jokes.explanation: Shows George Michael's growing manipulation skills while maintaining plausible deniability. Subtle character development humor.

jokes.text: That is a device that older people use to put their shoes on... without bending over! Get Shoedini right this second.
jokes.setup: Rebel asking about a supposed sex toy
jokes.punchline: It's actually an as-seen-on-TV shoe helper, complete with commercial dialogue
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that subverts both sexual expectations and commercial parody. The shift to commercial language is well-executed.

jokes.text: Which was the line George Michael had planned to use, so he improvised. Fine, but I hope we can spend some quality time together later.
jokes.setup: George Michael's plan being ruined by someone else using his line
jokes.punchline: His 'improvisation' being the exact opposite of what he wanted to say
jokes.explanation: Shows George Michael's inexperience with manipulation - when his plan fails, he does the opposite of what he intended. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Why don't you write a speech for her to say about you? When my mom's giving the speech, we'll be in the background, and we're going at it. And then, I open my mouth, and I have these rotted-out meth teeth
jokes.setup: Maeby planning to help her grandmother's campaign
jokes.punchline: Her plan involves fake meth teeth and pretending to ask for drug money
jokes.explanation: Classic Maeby - elaborate scheme with dark humor. The specificity of 'rotted-out meth teeth' and the family sabotage fits her character perfectly.

jokes.text: who had finally found a way to slip into the family. I'm out. I know. I get it. I feel like a moron.
jokes.setup: Narrator saying Tobias found a way into the family
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by him giving up and leaving
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing joke - the narrator's optimistic assessment immediately undermined by Tobias's defeat. Classic AD narrator/character contradiction.

jokes.text: 'always horny' shirts... eating chips in the car... 'Shut up, G.O.B.!' And almost making love to some roadside bushes. 'Shh. Don't tell Mom!'
jokes.setup: G.O.B. reading a touching father-son tribute speech
jokes.punchline: The increasingly inappropriate 'memories' including roadside bush romance
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from normal memories to deeply inappropriate ones. The fake intimacy and 'Shh. Don't tell Mom!' makes it wonderfully cringe.

jokes.text: I only wish it were real gold so that you wouldn't have 30 years of hard work ahead of you. Thirty years!
jokes.setup: G.O.B. giving his father a fake gold anchor
jokes.punchline: The 'gift' being a burden of 30 years of work, and George Sr.'s horrified reaction
jokes.explanation: Subverts the expectation of a heartfelt gift by making it literally burdensome. George Sr.'s reaction sells the horror of the 'gift.'

jokes.text: They'll have to pry it out of your cold, dead hands.
jokes.setup: George Sr. saying no one can take the anchor away
jokes.punchline: Lucille's dark twist on the NRA slogan, implying the anchor's weight could kill him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark wordplay that works on multiple levels - the literal weight of the anchor and the morbid twist on a famous phrase. Pure Lucille.

jokes.text: You're running to fill a House seat, not a sofa.
jokes.setup: Lindsay wearing inappropriate clothing for a political campaign
jokes.punchline: Lucille's cutting comparison between political office and furniture
jokes.explanation: Sharp Lucille insult with perfect structure. The House/sofa comparison is both specific and cutting, delivered with her signature cruelty.

jokes.text: Look who grew out of her sheet. I thought about it.
jokes.setup: Lindsay covering herself completely in a sheet
jokes.punchline: Lucille's sarcastic 'grew out of' as if Lindsay was going through a phase
jokes.explanation: Good visual setup with Lucille's condescending treatment of Lindsay's protest. The parent-child dynamic humor works well.

jokes.text: And without the ability to speak, they resorted to a language they'd learned long ago.
jokes.setup: Father and son too far apart to communicate verbally
jokes.punchline: They start doing elaborate hand gestures/sign language they apparently know
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment with visual comedy. The narrator's formal tone describing their secret communication adds layers to the father-son relationship.

jokes.text: I mean, well, no, technically, we forgot the debt. We can forget it, but we can never forgive it. We need the write-off.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explaining how they handled Michael's debt
jokes.punchline: The distinction between forgetting and forgiving for tax purposes
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that reveals character motivations. The business-minded distinction between emotional forgiveness and tax forgiveness is perfectly George Sr.

jokes.text: Maybe we should kiss again to teach them a lesson. Yeah? Okay, you're presenting awards on a crime scene
jokes.setup: Maeby and George Michael about to kiss
jokes.punchline: Interrupted by police officer pointing out they're at a crime scene
jokes.explanation: Good timing interrupt that shifts from cringe family dynamics to crime plot. The casual delivery of serious information is classic AD.

jokes.text: And my name is Buster Bluth, and, yes, I am a monster.
jokes.setup: Tobias in his backup costume after the banana stand disappears
jokes.punchline: Him declaring himself Buster and a monster while in costume
jokes.explanation: Tobias's accidental method acting creates confusion about the real Buster situation. His commitment to the bit adds to the chaos.

jokes.text: For the last time, I was not there. Or, if I was there, I have no memory.
jokes.setup: George Sr. denying he was at the pier
jokes.punchline: His immediate contradiction offering an alternative excuse
jokes.explanation: Classic George Sr. evasion pattern - absolute denial followed immediately by alternative excuse. Shows his instinctive dishonesty.

jokes.text: No, the only way you're stabbing anything is if you have a knife. That's what he meant.
jokes.setup: George Sr. threatening to stab guards to help Buster
jokes.punchline: Michael's literal clarification of the obvious stabbing requirement
jokes.explanation: Michael's practical clarification of his father's empty threat. The literalness of pointing out knife requirements deflates the tough talk.

jokes.text: Yes. And we found you in the break room with the Chanel man. He should never have lost his job for that.
jokes.setup: Buster remembering being lost at a department store
jokes.punchline: The implication that a Chanel employee was fired for helping lost Buster
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about collateral damage from the Bluth family's dysfunction. The casual mention of someone losing their job adds realistic consequences.

jokes.text: He was just giving me a Fanta! I know I should've spoken up for him, but I was only a boy. Barely out of my teens. I didn't... Currently.
jokes.setup: Buster explaining his regret about the fired employee
jokes.punchline: Michael's correction that Buster is currently barely out of his teens
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster characterization - his stunted development highlighted by Michael's deadpan correction. The 'Currently' is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: I said 'dead.' Maybe they said 'dead,' and I said, 'I wish.' Why'd you do that? I thought it was funny.
jokes.setup: Buster explaining what he told police about Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: His casual admission he thought joking about death was funny
jokes.explanation: Dark Buster humor showing his inappropriate responses to serious situations. His childlike reasoning for making death jokes is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: That's because if you saw the first video, you'd think, 'Why didn't that guy just erase this?' Some might consider that tampering with evidence.
jokes.setup: Buster tried to erase security footage but made it worse
jokes.punchline: The detective dryly explains the obvious legal problem
jokes.explanation: Solid deadpan delivery explaining Buster's obvious mistake, with good understatement

jokes.text: And the inmates hate me, 'cause what is a defense attorney doing in the shower?
jokes.setup: Barry explaining why he's unpopular in prison
jokes.punchline: The implication he's showering with inmates for legal confidentiality
jokes.explanation: Classic Barry incompetence with uncomfortable implications. His misunderstanding of legal confidentiality creates cringe humor.

jokes.text: Of course we're gonna have a great weekend.
jokes.setup: Michael asking if their legal position has been weakened
jokes.punchline: Barry completely mishearing 'greatly weakened' as 'great weekend'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barry mishearing that shows his incompetence. The optimistic response to serious legal concerns is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Uh, yeah, I guess I'll take a hamburger with two tops.
jokes.setup: Someone ordering food at what appears to be a party for Buster
jokes.punchline: Ordering a hamburger with two buns/tops instead of normal construction
jokes.explanation: Minor visual gag about the party preparations. Less character-specific but fits the overall absurdist tone.

jokes.text: You are just progressing by leaps and bounds. A victim of my own competence.
jokes.setup: Tobias being rejected as therapist and roommate
jokes.punchline: His delusional interpretation of rejection as therapeutic success
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias delusion - reframing complete rejection as professional success. 'Victim of my own competence' is peak Tobias self-aggrandizement.

jokes.text: This is my bastard son, Murphybrown. Murphy? And his last name is Brown? No. Murphybrown is his first name.
jokes.setup: Tobias introducing his supposed son
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous naming convention and TV show reference
jokes.explanation: Tobias's elaborate lie with characteristic over-explanation. The Murphy Brown reference and bastard son angle are both absurd and in character.

jokes.text: Close one! I think she's rather very hot. She okay?
jokes.setup: Learning Maeby is his half-sister
jokes.punchline: His immediate attraction followed by concern about the near-miss
jokes.explanation: Standard incest-adjacent humor for AD. The 'close one' reaction and immediate concern shows self-awareness of the family dysfunction.

jokes.text: So, you're saying I paid to bail you out? You weren't gonna leave me there, were you?
jokes.setup: Michael realizing he accidentally bailed out Barry instead of Buster
jokes.punchline: Barry's assumption that Michael would never abandon him
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the earlier miscommunication setup. Barry's touching faith in Michael's loyalty despite the mix-up adds character depth.

jokes.text: Why don't you get me a Fanta on your way out of the family.
jokes.setup: George Sr. dismissing Murphybrown from the family
jokes.punchline: The casual callback to Buster's earlier Fanta story while banishing someone
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that connects the earlier emotional story to current family rejection. The casual cruelty mixed with specific beverage request is perfect.
